knob_notes:
Voicing overview: Classic British rock — midforward and punchy. Single channel master volume amp; gain comes from preamp + master interaction.

Gain structure:
- 0-3: Cleaner with edge of breakup at higher master.
- 4-6: Sweet spot for classic rock crunch.
- 7-10: Saturated rock/metal rhythm. Compresses heavily past 8.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: Tight; safe up to 6. Above 7 gets boomy in higher gain.
- Mid: The character knob. 6-8 is the JCM800 honk. Below 4 it loses identity.
- Treble: Runs hot. 4-6 typical; above 7 gets harsh.
- Presence: Power-section bite. Use 5-7 to keep it cutting.

Common starting points:
- Classic rock rhythm: G5 B5 M7 T5 P5
- 80s metal rhythm: G7 B5 M6 T5 P6 + OD pedal in front for tightness
- Lead boost: G7 B4 M8 T6 P7

Pairing notes: Loves humbuckers; single coils sit thinner. TS-style OD in front (low drive, high level) is the canonical tightening trick.

Voicing overview: The grandfather of British rock — non-master volume, gain comes from cranking the volume into power-tube saturation. Open, dynamic, touch-sensitive.

Gain structure:
- Volume 0-3: Pristine clean.
- Volume 4-6: Edge of breakup; cleans up with picking dynamics and guitar volume.
- Volume 7-10: Saturated classic rock; heavily power-tube driven.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: Add to taste; rarely past 6 since power-tube saturation already adds low-end thickness.
- Mid: Counter-clockwise scoop is unusual on Plexi — it's traditionally cranked.
- Treble: 6-8 typical; high-treble channel input is brighter.
- Presence: 5-8; power section emphasis.

Common starting points:
- Bluesy rock: V6 (Bridged: Vol1+Vol2 high) B4 M6 T6 P5
- Classic rock crank: V8 B5 M7 T7 P6 — needs an attenuator at home volumes
- Solo cleanup: V9 + roll guitar volume to 5-7 for clean

Pairing notes: PAF humbuckers and single coils both work. Treble booster in front is the EVH/Brian May trick. Attenuator essentially required indoors.

Voicing overview: Tight, scooped modern high-gain. Vintage mode is more open and midforward; Modern mode is tighter and more compressed with a cabinet-thumping low-end signature.

Gain structure:
- 0-3: Rarely used; Clean channel preferred.
- 4-7: Sweet spot for rock/metal rhythm; harmonics bloom around 6.
- 7-10: Saturated lead. Above 8, noise gate essential.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: Very responsive. Keep below 5 for tight palm mutes. Above 6 gets flubby in drop tunings.
- Mid: Counter-intuitive taper — 9-3 o'clock usable, past 3 gets nasal/muddy. 'Scoop' at 9-10 o'clock for modern metal.
- Treble: Runs hot. 5-6 bright; above 7 ice-picks with single coils.
- Presence: Independent of Treble; cabinet-level cut/clarity.

Common starting points:
- Modern metal rhythm (Modern mode): G7 B4 M5 T6 P7
- Classic hard rock (Vintage mode): G5 B5 M6 T6 P5
- Lead boost: G8 B4 M7 T7 P6 + OD pedal for tightness

Pairing notes: Loves humbuckers and active pickups. Single coils above T6 ice-pick. TS-style OD in front (low drive, high level) tightens low-end.

Voicing overview: Three-channel monster with a cult-favorite midforward lead voicing. Graphic EQ post-preamp is a critical part of the iconic Mark sound (the 'V shape' EQ).

Gain structure:
- Lead Drive 4-6: Singing lead with sustain and harmonics.
- Lead Drive 7-10: Compressed shred. High Master for power-tube interaction.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: Pre-EQ; keep low (3-4) for clarity. The graphic EQ adds bass back at 80Hz/240Hz.
- Mid: This control is interactive with Treble — they share frequency. Hetfield-style scoop = mid 0, treble 7.
- Treble: 6-8 normal. With mid at 0, treble drives both mid and high.
- Presence: 5-8 for cut.
- Graphic EQ: The classic Mark scoop is V-shape (boost 80Hz, 750Hz, 6.6kHz; cut 240Hz, 2.2kHz).

Common starting points:
- Hetfield rhythm (Lead ch): G6 B3 M0 T7 P5, EQ V-shape
- Lukather lead (Lead ch): G7 B4 M5 T6 P6, EQ flat
- Crunch (Rhythm 2): G5 B5 M5 T6 P5

Pairing notes: Active pickups push the front end harder. Less reliant on OD pedals than a Recto.

Voicing overview: The benchmark for loud, sparkly American clean. Massive headroom — breaks up only at very high volumes. The Vibrato channel has tube reverb and tremolo.

Gain structure:
- Volume 0-4: Pristine clean.
- Volume 5-7: Edge of breakup (still very clean; needs an OD pedal for serious dirt).
- Volume 8-10: Power-tube saturation, but at deafening volumes.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: 3-5 normal. Above 6 gets boomy in larger rooms.
- Mid: Inherently scooped voicing. Mid knob mostly subtractive — 5 is neutral.
- Treble: 5-7 typical. Above 8 gets brittle with bright pickups.
- Reverb: Lush spring; 3-5 most usable.

Common starting points:
- Country/jazz clean: V4 B5 M5 T6 R3
- SRV blues clean: V7 B5 M5 T7 R4
- Pedal platform: V5 B4 M5 T5 R3 (low so pedals shape the tone)

Pairing notes: Pedal-friendly clean platform. Bright pickups can fatigue ears past T7. Strats and Teles are the canonical pairing; humbuckers work but lose some 'jangle'.

Voicing overview: The studio standard for warm Fender clean and edge-of-breakup. Lower headroom than Twin — natural tube saturation at gigging volumes.

Gain structure:
- Volume 0-4: Pristine clean.
- Volume 5-7: Sweet edge-of-breakup; cleans up with picking dynamics.
- Volume 8-10: Power tube saturation; classic Fender lead voice.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: 3-5; the amp is naturally bassy with humbuckers, scoop bass with HH.
- Treble: 5-7 normal; above 8 brittle with single coils.
- Reverb: Short, classic spring; 2-5 most usable.

Note: No mid or presence knob — mids are fixed by circuit voicing.

Common starting points:
- SRV clean: V6 B5 T6 R3
- Country chicken pickin': V5 B4 T7 R4
- Edge of breakup: V7 B5 T6 R3 — roll guitar volume for cleanup

Pairing notes: Single coils sing on this amp. Humbuckers can sound thick — drop bass to 3-4. Loves OD pedals (TS-style) for higher gain.

Voicing overview: The tweed-era amp that inspired the Marshall JTM45 — touch-sensitive, dynamic, midforward unlike later Fenders.

Gain structure:
- Volume 0-4: Clean.
- Volume 5-7: Edge of breakup; dynamic tube grit.
- Volume 8-10: Saturated tweed roar; classic blues rock.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: Low (3-5); the amp is naturally warm.
- Mid: 6-8 the tweed honk.
- Treble: 5-7; brighter than later black-face Fenders.
- Presence: 4-7; adds power-section bite.

Common starting points:
- Blues clean: V5 B5 M6 T6 P5
- Roaring tweed lead: V8 B4 M7 T6 P6
- Pedal platform: V4 B4 M6 T5 P4

Pairing notes: Loves both single coils and humbuckers; jumper the bright + normal channels for unique blends. Roy Buchanan / Larry Carlton classic tone.

Voicing overview: The British chime — bright, jangly, crystalline. Top Boost channel adds the iconic high-end shimmer.

Gain structure:
- Volume 0-4: Pristine chimey clean.
- Volume 5-7: Edge of breakup with EL84 grind.
- Volume 8-10: Saturated AC30 crunch — Brian May territory.

EQ interactions:
- Top Boost Bass + Treble: These are cut-only EQs (passive). Higher = more.
- Bass: 4-6; the amp is bright by design — adding bass tames it.
- Treble: 4-6; above 7 gets shrill.
- Tone Cut (master): Cuts high frequencies; counter-clockwise = brighter.

Common starting points:
- The Edge clean: V5 B5 T5, Cut 4 + delay
- Beatles jangle: V7 B4 T6, Cut 3
- Brian May: V9 B5 T6, Cut 2 + treble booster in front

Pairing notes: Brilliant with single coils (especially Strats and Ricks). Humbuckers sometimes thick — back off bass. Treble booster (Rangemaster-style) is the classic Brian May front-end.

Voicing overview: Thick, midforward British tone — distinctly Orange. Two channels (clean + dirty) with shared EQ.

Gain structure:
- 0-3: Edge crunch; not super clean even at 0.
- 4-7: Sweet rock/stoner/doom rhythm.
- 7-10: Saturated thick lead. Compresses past 8.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: 5-7; the amp loves low-end and stays tight unlike US scooped amps.
- Mid: 5-7 the Orange honk; below 4 loses identity.
- Treble: 4-6; above 7 gets harsh.
- Reverb: Spring-like, 2-4.

Common starting points:
- Stoner rock: G6 B6 M6 T5 R0
- Heavy rock rhythm: G7 B6 M6 T5 R0
- Doom: G8 B7 M5 T4 R2

Pairing notes: Loves humbuckers and P90s. Drop tunings sound massive. Less pedal-dependent than other amps — the dirty channel handles most rock duties on its own.

Voicing overview: Modern metal benchmark — tight, aggressive, with brutal low-end. Three channels; Lead channel is the iconic high-gain voice.

Gain structure:
- Lead 4-6: Hard rock rhythm.
- Lead 6-8: Modern metal rhythm — tight palm mutes.
- Lead 8-10: Lead saturation; needs aggressive noise gate.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: 4-6; tight by design but above 7 gets flubby.
- Mid: 5-7 the EVH-tinged honk; scooped (3-4) for modern djent.
- Treble: 5-7; above 8 ice-picks.
- Presence: 5-7; adds cabinet-level bite.
- Resonance: Power-section low-end thump; 5-7 typical.

Common starting points:
- Modern metal rhythm: G6 B5 M5 T6 P6 + Resonance 6
- Djent: G6 B4 M3 T6 P7 + TS-style OD in front
- Lead boost: G8 B4 M6 T7 P7 + boost pedal

Pairing notes: Active pickups (EMG 81) the canonical pairing. TS-style OD in front (low drive) is mandatory for djent tightness. Less responsive to picking dynamics than older designs.

Voicing overview: Boutique three-channel monster — Green clean, Blue Marshall-flavored crunch, Red modern high-gain. Rich harmonic complexity throughout.

Gain structure:
- Blue 4-6: Plexi-style classic rock crunch.
- Blue 7-10: JCM800-ish saturated lead.
- Red 4-7: Modern rock/metal rhythm.
- Red 8-10: Saturated lead with sustain.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: Tight; 5-6 typical.
- Mid: 5-7; complex midrange voicing rewards careful dialing.
- Treble: 5-7; above 8 harsh.
- Presence: 5-7.
- Excursion (front-panel mini-toggle): tightens low-end further.

Common starting points:
- Plexi-style (Blue): G7 B5 M6 T6 P6
- Modern rock (Red): G6 B5 M5 T6 P6
- Lead (Red): G8 B4 M6 T7 P7

Pairing notes: Single coils and humbuckers both shine. Less compressed than American high-gain amps. Famous for its three-dimensional 'air' that survives the mix.

Voicing overview: Modern hot-rodded Marshall — what a JCM800 wishes it could be at modern gain levels. Tight, articulate, lead-friendly.

Gain structure:
- BE 4-6: Hard rock rhythm.
- BE 6-9: Modern metal rhythm with Marshall DNA.
- HBE: Higher gain version with more compression.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: 5-6; very responsive, tight low-end.
- Mid: 6-8 the Marshall midforward signature.
- Treble: 5-7.
- Presence: 6-8 for cut.

Common starting points:
- Modern hard rock: G6 B5 M7 T6 P6
- Lead boost: G7 B5 M7 T6 P7
- Tight metal: G8 B4 M6 T6 P7 + minor OD in front

Pairing notes: Loves both passive humbuckers and PAFs. The tightness lets it work in modern metal contexts where a vintage Marshall would flub.

Voicing overview: Eddie Van Halen's spec for the smaller 5150 III — same brown sound DNA as the 100W but with more useful master volumes for home/studio.

Gain structure:
- Lead 4-6: Modern hard rock.
- Lead 6-8: VH-style brown sound rhythm.
- Lead 8-10: Saturated lead.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: 5-6 typical; the 50W has slightly less low-end thump than 100W.
- Mid: 5-7 brown sound midrange.
- Treble: 5-7.
- Presence: 5-7.

Common starting points:
- VH brown sound: G7 B5 M6 T6 P6
- Modern rhythm: G6 B5 M5 T6 P6
- Lead boost: G8 B5 M7 T7 P7 + EP Booster

Pairing notes: Single coils + humbuckers both work; classic pairing is a Frankenstrat-style HSS.

Voicing overview: 90s evolution of the JCM800 — brighter, tighter, more gain on tap. Diode clipping in the gain stage divides opinion: some find it harsh, others love the consistency.

Gain structure:
- 4-6: Hard rock rhythm.
- 6-9: 90s rock and grunge rhythm.
- 9-10: Saturated lead.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: 4-6; tighter than JCM800.
- Mid: 5-7.
- Treble: 4-6; above 7 harsh.
- Presence: 5-7.

Common starting points:
- 90s alt rock rhythm: G7 B5 M6 T5 P6
- Grunge: G8 B5 M5 T5 P6
- Lead: G9 B4 M7 T6 P7

Pairing notes: Humbuckers preferred. TS-style OD in front smooths the diode harshness.

Voicing overview: Boutique German four-channel monster — extreme tightness, modern saturation. Channel 4 is the iconic high-gain voice (Tool / NIN / Peter Gabriel band staple).

Gain structure:
- Ch3 4-7: Hard rock to modern metal rhythm.
- Ch4 4-7: Saturated lead with tight low-end.
- Ch4 7-10: Brutal modern metal.

EQ interactions:
- Bass: 4-6; extremely tight by design.
- Mid: 4-6; the VH4 is naturally midforward.
- Treble: 4-6.
- Presence: 5-7.
- Deep (back panel): adds resonance.

Common starting points:
- Modern metal rhythm (Ch3): G7 B5 M5 T5 P6
- Lead (Ch4): G6 B5 M5 T5 P6
- Brutal djent (Ch4): G8 B4 M4 T5 P7

Pairing notes: Active pickups push it well. Ridiculously tight even without an OD pedal in front. The cleanest of the modern high-gain amps.
